title: Side Quest Hooks (Fantasy)
description: Incidental hooks that emerge from the environment, NPCs, or overheard conversation — small enough to ignore, interesting enough not to.

entries.result: A wanted notice posted near the town gate names someone the party has already met. The crime listed doesn't match their behavior.
A child is sitting outside a building, waiting for someone who hasn't come out in three days. The child insists everything is fine.
A merchant sells the party something they later realize was reported stolen in the last town. The merchant is long gone.
An old map found in a dead man's belongings shows a building that no longer exists at the location marked — or does it?
A local healer is running low on a critical herb and quietly asks the party if they happened to pass through the valley where it grows.
A ghost is haunting an inn but only seems frightened, not threatening. It keeps pointing at the wall.
Two rival craftspeople are selling nearly identical goods. One is clearly copying the other but denies it. The original creator wants help proving it.
A traveling friar is being followed by someone who keeps just out of sight. The friar seems to know about it and refuses to explain.
A shrine to a local deity has been defaced. The priest is furious. The locals suspect each other. The truth involves neither.
An elderly woman is offering a significant reward for the return of a cat. The cat, when found, is clearly not an ordinary animal.
A bridge toll collector has been pocketing more than their share. The road authority wants the matter handled quietly, without involving the lord.
A local legend says treasure is buried beneath a specific tree. Seventeen people have dug there. The eighteenth attempt finds something — but not treasure.
A flock of birds has been circling a specific farmhouse for two days. The farmer says nothing is wrong. The birds suggest otherwise.
A young noble is secretly trying to buy their way out of an arranged marriage and needs discreet intermediaries.
An herbalist's shop was robbed — not of money, but of three very specific plants with no obvious connection. The herbalist is scared.
A festival is about to begin and the master of ceremonies has gone missing. The understudy knows the rituals but not the passwords required to open the vault of ceremonial items.
A letter arrives at an inn addressed to one of the party members — but it's in a hand none of them recognize and predates their arrival by a week.
A well in the center of town has started producing water that smells faintly wrong. Nobody has gotten sick yet.
A local militia is looking for a deserter from their ranks. The description matches a person the party helped three days ago.
A road-side shrine has fresh offerings placed on it every morning. The nearest settlement is two days away.
A blacksmith keeps turning down work despite clearly needing coin. They seem frightened of making anything that could be used as a weapon.
A traveling tinker is selling items that can't possibly have come from where they claim they came from.
An entire street of houses has been quietly vacated. The neighbors say nothing happened. Their eyes say otherwise.
A child shows the party a tunnel entrance they found while playing. The tunnel is old, reinforced, and has footprints going in that don't come back out.
A retired soldier is being pressured by their former officers to return to service. They don't want to go and aren't allowed to say why.
A local mill has stopped operating and the miller's family is pretending everything is normal while quietly leaving town in small groups.
A beekeeper's hives have gone silent. The beekeeper says bees don't do that. Not in this region. Not at this time of year.
A message board has a notice written in a language that nobody in town reads — except the party, who realize it's a warning.
A traveling bard is performing a ballad that describes, in veiled terms, an event the party was present for. They didn't tell anyone.
An aqueduct that supplies half the city has a crack in it that the engineers say will take a month to fix. Someone has offered to fix it in a day, for a price.
A pawnbroker has acquired a piece of jewelry that is clearly part of a set. The other pieces have been missing for a decade, since a robbery.
A merchant caravan has stopped at the crossroads and refuses to proceed. Their leader says they're waiting for a sign but won't elaborate.
A fire destroyed a warehouse last season. The owner was compensated, rebuilt quickly, and was clearly not upset. An investigator thinks the fire was deliberate.
A series of buildings in a neighborhood all have the same symbol scratched into their doorframes. Nobody admits to putting it there.
A forest path the party is using has been blocked by a freshly felled tree. It's too large to have fallen naturally and the cut is clean.
A local lord has issued a decree banning the hunting of a specific animal in a specific wood, effective immediately, with no explanation.
A pair of siblings are arguing over an inheritance. One is clearly lying about what the will says. The other doesn't have a copy of the will.
An alchemist's shop has been closed for a week with a 'back soon' sign. The neighbors haven't seen anyone go in or out.
A fishing village reports that their nets keep coming up torn, in the same spot, on the same days of the week.
A traveling physician is healing people at a rate that strains belief. No one is questioning it because they're grateful. One person is questioning it.
An old watchtower outside town has been relit for the first time in twenty years. The authorities deny having ordered it.
A sculptor's recent work depicts faces no one in town recognizes. The sculptor claims they're from dreams. The faces appear to be real people.
A harvest festival is scheduled but the crops this year are strange — not failed, just different from what was planted.
A tollbooth on a river crossing has been abandoned mid-shift. The toll-keeper's belongings are still there.
A respected elder has begun giving away their possessions, quietly and without explanation. They are not ill.
Two travelers arrived at the same inn on the same night and have been watching each other without speaking. Both become agitated when the party asks about them.
A local herbalist has reported that something has eaten all the nightshade in a very specific acre of forest. Nothing eats nightshade.
A new road is being cut through land that locals insist should not be disturbed. The workers have started refusing shifts but won't say why.
A census collector hasn't returned from a routine survey of the outer villages. The route is not dangerous. Or wasn't.
A river ford that the party uses regularly has shifted. The bottom has changed. Something large moved through.
An inn's famous cook has suddenly quit, and the inn's regulars are more upset about this than seems reasonable.
A minor festival honoring a local saint has been canceled without announcement. The temple doors are locked.
A horse has been found wandering miles from the nearest stable. It's well-bred, well-shod, and had a rider recently.
A shepherd's flock has been returning to the fold each night minus exactly one animal. No tracks. No blood. No wool.
A dock worker overheard a cargo manifest that didn't match the crates being unloaded. They want someone to check before the ship leaves tomorrow.
A newly appointed magistrate is asking questions about a case closed fifteen years ago. Several people are very interested in who is asking.
A house at the edge of town is known locally as unlucky. The latest tenants have moved in anyway and seem fine. Three weeks later, they've stopped answering the door.
A town well has produced a strange object — smooth, worked, and old — brought up with the bucket. Nobody claims it.
A courier service is missing a rider on a route that should have taken two days. A week has passed.
A book in the local library has been checked out sixty times in the last year by sixty different people. Nobody knows why it's popular.
A refugee camp outside the city walls has grown faster than expected. The new arrivals are from a place nobody has heard of.
A miller has found strange marks ground into their millstone. The marks weren't there when the stone was set.
A travelling pair of merchants are asking detailed questions about the location of every well and cistern in the region. For a survey, they say.
A raven has followed the party for two days and seems to understand when it's being discussed.
A gravestone in the local cemetery is for someone who the party has just spoken to — and the date of death is next week.
A dyer reports that their vats have been producing the wrong colors for two weeks, despite using the same materials and process.
A market stall is selling produce labeled as coming from a village that burned down three years ago.
A retired navigator has a chart of an inland area marked with sea-chart conventions. They refuse to explain where it came from.
A set of footprints leading into a marsh stops abruptly in the middle of a clearing. No return prints. No sign of struggle.
A guardsman is clearly afraid of someone in the town but won't file a report or admit it directly.
A chandler is making candles of an unusual shape and size. Every order is for the same specification, all from different buyers.
A clockmaker's timepieces in a specific neighborhood all run fast. Not by the same amount. In the same direction.
A funeral is being held for someone who, according to official records, died two years ago.
A trader is offering premium prices for used clothing from a specific decade. No explanation given.
A tower in the middle of a field has no door. It has never had a door. A light was seen inside it last night.
A song has become popular in the city but nobody knows who wrote it. The lyrics, listened to carefully, describe a crime in progress.
A monastery has started turning away travelers who have been welcome for generations. The abbot has changed the policy and will not explain.
A glassblower has been commissioned to make the same item forty times over. Each one is slightly different. They don't know who ordered them or why.
A child is drawing maps of underground spaces they claim to have never visited.
A noblewoman's portrait in the great hall has been quietly altered. The original subject's face has been replaced. The staff pretend not to notice.
A traveling circus has arrived. One of the performers is clearly trying to get someone's attention without being seen doing so.
A local historian has gone silent after publishing a pamphlet about the founding of the town. The pamphlets have been removed from shelves.
A lock on a public building has been changed without announcement. Nobody official will admit to authorizing it.
A cartwright has found unusual material woven into the axle of a wagon they were hired to repair. The material is not wood or metal.
An auction house is selling an estate's contents. One item — a plain iron box — has a reserve price twenty times higher than everything else combined.
A road has been rerouted around a specific field. Officially, due to drainage. Locals say the field changed two months ago.
A tanner's apprentice has gone missing. The tanner filed no report and seems relieved. The apprentice's family is not relieved.
A storm last month deposited a piece of wreckage from a ship far inland. The ship's name is still readable. It was declared lost at sea four years ago.
A stonecutter found words carved inside a wall during renovation. The words are in a language older than the building.
A series of identical trinkets have appeared in lost-and-found boxes across the city. Nobody claims them. Nobody admits to losing them.
A merchant's ledger found by accident contains a coded section. Decoded, it's a list of names and dates — all future dates.
A local eccentric has built something in their yard under a tarpaulin and refuses all questions about it. The neighbors smell ozone at night.
A constable has been asking about the party specifically. Not to speak to them — just asking about them.
An abandoned homestead on the party's route has been recently cleaned, aired, and stocked — as if someone expects to return.
A wanted criminal has apparently died of old age in their sleep in a rented room. The body is forty years too old for the age on the poster.
A public fountain has been producing water with a distinctive taste that locals claim gives them unusually clear dreams.
A letter arrives at the inn for someone by a name none of the party uses. The description inside is accurate.
A shepherd found a pit in the hills with worked stone steps descending into it. They sealed it with logs and came to town to tell someone.
A traveling merchant keeps returning to the same town with the same goods — which never seem to diminish no matter how much they sell.
A child has found a door in the woods. Not attached to anything. Just standing there. They want someone to open it with them.
A widow has hired three different investigators to find out what happened to her husband. All three took the money and stopped reporting back.
A traveling scholar is mapping ruins no one locally has heard of — except the ruins are described exactly right for a place the party knows.
A traveling player's troupe has lost their lead performer. Their understudy knows the lines but won't perform. The party gets pressed into service.
A game of skill at the market fair has been won by the same person for seven years. No one has accused them of cheating, but no one has beaten them either.
A local farmer has found a set of remains on their land and wants them dealt with before the authorities ask how long they knew.
A courier has been given a message to deliver but the addressee is dead. They can't return to sender. They need someone to decide what to do with it.
A traveling holy person is performing miracles at the edge of town. A local priest wants someone to verify whether the miracles are genuine or theatrical.
A book has been passed person to person through this town for sixty years. Each owner adds a page. The current owner died leaving it to someone they'd never met.
A newly installed lord is making decisions that seem random but follow a pattern the steward has noticed and is afraid to report up the chain.
A glass eye found in the road is identified by a physician as coming from their former mentor, who disappeared seven years ago.
A strange star has been visible for three nights in a position where no star should be. The local astronomer won't comment officially.
A building in town has been bought and sold eight times in three years. Each sale was at a loss. Each buyer claimed they were moving on.
A set of handprints in dried clay has been found in a riverbank. They're too large to be human and too deliberate to be accidental.
A musician at the tavern plays a specific tune at closing time that makes animals within hearing distance agitated.
A former noble's servant is trying to sell information about their former employer but is afraid to say what the information is until they know who's buying.
Two identical letters were sent to two different people in town on the same day. Each claims to be the only recipient.
A body found in the river shows no sign of drowning. The river is how they arrived, not how they died.
A prayer to a local saint is rumored to be answered, but only in inconvenient ways. Three people have tried it. Three people regret it.
A child prodigy is being quietly moved from one private tutor to the next. Each tutor ends their engagement without explanation.
A town crier has started adding a single extra sentence to the end of each official announcement. The additions make no sense. No one has told them to stop.
A map in an old estate shows a road through land now occupied by forest. The road is not on any current map. The forest is old.
A local crime lord's enforcer has apparently gone legitimate. Rivals are circling. The enforcer asks the party to make it look like they're still dangerous without doing anything illegal.
A holy relic has been lent between two temples for decades. Each claims the other has the original and they have a copy.
A widow's house keeps catching fire in the same corner of the same room, never spreading, never leaving soot. The widow seems more annoyed than frightened.
A tailor made a suit for a client who never came to collect it. The tailor keeps the suit on display. People who see it report dreaming about the person who commissioned it.
A farmer's dog has started herding the wrong animals — specifically, the neighbors' chickens — into a pattern that looks like letters.
A locked chest washed up on shore contains only a name and an address. The address is real. The name belongs to someone who has been in prison for a decade.
A bridge is being repaired. Workers report finding human bones in the original foundations — too many and too deliberate to be accidental.
A noble family's crest has recently changed. The heralds say it was always this way. The older servants say it wasn't.
A street preacher has been saying the same thing every day for a year. Today they said something different. Then they stopped preaching entirely.
A boat found drifting has no one aboard, a full cargo, and a name that matches a vessel reported sunk in a storm two years ago.
A local alchemist is buying up all available red clay from every source in the region. They won't say what it's for.
A traveling merchant has a route that takes them through the most isolated regions of the area. They've been doing it for years. They're always in perfect health.
A new symbol has started appearing on guild notices — not any official guild sigil. Members receiving the notices deny seeing it.
A landowner has stopped collecting rent from their tenants but is not selling the land. The tenants are grateful and confused in equal measure.
A crossroads inn has a room permanently rented by someone who hasn't been seen in months. The staff leave fresh food there each day.
A prisoner has escaped from the city gaol — not through force, not through magic that anyone detected, simply through a door that was definitely locked.
A particular spring has been producing water at twice its normal rate for six weeks. Geologically speaking, this should not be possible.
An entire flock of migratory birds has settled in the city and refuses to leave. Locals say this happened once before, right before the old disaster.
A city official's signature has been appearing on documents the official has no memory of signing.
A potter's apprentice has been selling pieces on the side — pieces of extraordinary quality they claim to make in their sleep.
A caravan road is being watched — not by bandits, but by stationary observers who retreat when approached and don't appear threatening.
A specific type of stone is going missing from building sites across the city. Not valuably, not obviously — just quietly absent by morning.
A census taker found that one household has been occupied by the same family for two hundred years without a single recorded death.
A local smith received a commission for a very specific set of tools from an anonymous buyer. The tools have no obvious trade use.
An elder's memories have begun arriving before the events they remember. They are frightened and asking for someone discreet.
A professional duellist has refused all challenges for a month. Their reputation is suffering. They refuse to explain.
A road crew has been widening the king's road and has found, beneath the gravel, an older road — better built, going in a different direction.
A retired smuggler has legitimate business now. Someone from their past has arrived, not threatening — just watching. Waiting.
A village has been tithing to a temple for generations. A traveling accountant notices the temple they've been paying doesn't exist.
A spy has been feeding accurate information to three different factions, all of whom believe they are the only recipient.
A famous warrior's grave has been found empty. The coffin is intact. The warrior has been dead for thirty years.
A merchant's strongbox was robbed but only one coin was taken — an old commemorative piece with no trade value.
A healer has been keeping a patient alive past all expectation. The patient wants to die. The healer won't explain why they won't let them.
A town's founding charter has been recalled for 'archival review' by a distant authority. The charter contains rights the town's residents depend on.
A tavern game — simple, popular — has been producing outcomes that defy probability for two weeks. The proprietor is afraid to stop hosting it.
A local wise woman has posted a notice offering to answer any question, truthfully, once, to anyone who can find her door.
A particular street musician plays only songs from a generation ago. They claim to be that generation's age. They look thirty.
A guild seal is being used by someone not in the guild. The guild wants it back. The user wants to explain before they surrender it.
A barn on the outskirts of town lights up from within each night at the same time, then goes dark. No one will say what's inside.
A missing persons notice describes someone who matches the party's description almost exactly — except for one detail that is subtly, deliberately wrong.
A wine merchant has been selling the same vintage for eight years, claiming new stock from the same vineyard. The vineyard burned six years ago.
A traveling herbalist's cart is full of plants that, according to the region's climate, cannot have grown within a week's travel.
A scribe has been quietly correcting the official town records — not falsifying, just making them more accurate. Someone in power is furious.
A child has come to town alone, asking for someone by name. The name is the party's contact. The child won't say who sent them.
A tavern has a room that guests report having strange dreams in. The proprietor charges extra for it. It is always booked.
An old soldier's memorial has been quietly defaced and restored three times. Nobody saw who did either.
A traveling tinker is selling repaired goods of exceptional quality — but the repairs are invisible. Not well-hidden. Literally invisible.
A local official is clearly being blackmailed. They're equally clearly not going to admit it. Something needs to change before the next council vote.
A child's game in the district involves a rhyme that describes, in children's language, what sounds like a genuine historical massacre.
A messenger pigeon arrived at a loft without a message. Instead, there's a very small key attached to its leg.
A priest refuses to grant last rites to a dying person. The dying person is terrified. The priest is also terrified.
A river has changed its smell in a way that the local fisherfolk say means something is different upstream. They refuse to go look.
A pair of boots left outside a traveler's door each morning have been clean and mended each morning. The traveler has never seen who does it.
An estate sale includes a painting whose subject is unmistakably a person currently living — painted two hundred years ago.
A constable keeps returning to the scene of a solved crime. They've begun to think they solved the wrong crime.
A rat catcher has found something in the tunnels beneath the city that they will not describe but that has made them afraid of the dark for the first time.
A guild treasurer has discovered a discrepancy that predates any living member. The original ledger shows money paid to an account no one recognizes.
A ferry operator has been letting one passenger cross free each trip — a specific passenger who is never the same person twice but always sits in the same seat.
A local baker's bread has started causing extremely pleasant and very consistent dreams. They want to know what changed — one ingredient was swapped by accident last month.
A city gate has a shadow that falls in the wrong direction. Engineers say it's an optical illusion. They won't meet each other's eyes when they say it.
A new tenant in a house near the party's lodgings moves nothing in, makes no noise, and has received twenty visitors in three days at odd hours.
A fire investigator has been quietly reconstructing an official 'accident' fire and believes it was deliberate. They're afraid of who they might implicate.
An apprentice weaver has produced a tapestry that depicts, in remarkable detail, an event that hasn't happened yet.
A horse trader is selling a specific animal at a loss to get rid of it. The horse is in perfect health. The trader won't say why they want it gone.
A child is collecting door knockers — specifically ones shaped like a particular creature — and won't say why. They've gathered eleven.
A veteran city messenger has refused three routes to the same district in the past month. They've taken the pay cuts without explanation.
A traveling theater company performs the same play each night but with different outcomes. They claim it's audience choice. The audience disagrees.
A woman has been looking for her brother for two years. She just found him. He doesn't remember her.
A lantern at a crossroads has never gone out in recorded memory. This week it went out. The locals are waiting for something to happen.
A scholar's notes reference a text that no library admits owning. The notes suggest it was common knowledge a century ago.
A blind seer is giving advice to anyone who asks. Their advice is accurate. They ask only one question in return: what will you do with it?

A long-running neighborhood dispute over a fence line has escalated to the point where a minor official was assaulted. It needs to be resolved before it becomes a legal matter neither party can afford.
A wax seal appears on a building's door each morning and is gone by noon. Each seal is different. Each depicts a different creature.
A traveling doctor is offering a free cure for a widespread local ailment. The cure works. The doctor wants nothing. The town elder is suspicious.
A letter delivered to the wrong address contains information about a crime that hasn't happened yet. The original recipient's address is wrong in a way that looks deliberate.
A mill's grinding wheel produces a sound that, locals claim, has been repeating the same irregular rhythm for thirty years. A musician has worked out that it's a song.
A map seller is offering a chart of the underworld — the literal underground, not the metaphorical one — in remarkable detail, for a price that seems far too low.
A local dignitary is attending every funeral in the city, regardless of whether they knew the deceased. They're looking for someone.
The party's own names appear in a list found in a dead man's pocket. Alongside four other names, three of which have been crossed out.
